sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After clicking blue at (56, 38):
- Charcoal at (49,41)-(50,43) moved to (47,41)-(48,43) - moved up by 2 rows
- Purple at (51,41)-(51,43) moved to (49,41)-(49,43) - moved up by 2 rows
So clicking the blue block in the fourth column moved the blocks in that column up by 2 rows. The pattern is clear: each blue block controls the column it's in, and clicking it moves the colored blocks in that column up by 2 rows.
Now I need to figure out the goal. Looking at the current state:
- Leftmost column (cols 8-10): charcoal at (49,8)-(50,10), green at (51,8)-(51,10)
- Second column (cols 14-15): green at (41,14)-(41,15)
- Third column (cols 36-37): purple at (39,36)-(39,37)
- Fourth column (cols 41-43): charcoal at (47,41)-(48,43), purple at (49,41)-(49,43)
- Fifth column (cols 48-54): yellow at (47,48)-(47,49), charcoal at (27,52)-(28,54), yellow at (29,52)-(29,54)
The goal might be to move all colored blocks to the same height, or to align them with the black horizontal bars. Looking at the image, I see black horizontal bars at different heights. Maybe the goal is to move the colored blocks so they align with these bars.
Let me try clicking the blue blocks in all columns multiple times to move the blocks up, and see if there's a pattern or target position.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, I see:
- The leftmost column has a green block on top of a charcoal block
- The fourth column has a purple block on top of a charcoal block
- The fifth column has a yellow block on top of a charcoal block
- The second and third columns have single green and purple blocks respectively
The black horizontal bars might be targets. Let me try to move all blocks up until they reach the top of their columns or align with the bars.
Let me continue clicking the blue blocks to move the blocks up. I'll click each blue block multiple times.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Clicking blue blocks moves colored blocks in that column up by 2 rows.
# Need to figure out the target positions.
# Let me click all blue blocks multiple times to move blocks up.

# Blue blocks at: (56,12), (56,16), (56,24), (56,28), (56,34), (56,38), (56,46), (56,50)
# But some columns don't have colored blocks. Let me identify which columns have blocks.

# Current colored block positions:
# - Col 8-10: charcoal at (49,8)-(50,10), green at (51,8)-(51,10) -> blue at (56,12)
# - Col 14-15: green at (41,14)-(41,15) -> blue at (56,16)
# - Col 36-37: purple at (39,36)-(39,37) -> blue at (56,34)
# - Col 41-43: charcoal at (47,41)-(48,43), purple at (49,41)-(49,43) -> blue at (56,38)
# - Col 48-49: yellow at (47,48)-(47,49) -> blue at (56,46)
# - Col 52-54: charcoal at (27,52)-(28,54), yellow at (29,52)-(29,54) -> blue at (56,50)

# Let me click all relevant blue blocks to move blocks up
# Try clicking each blue block once to see the effect
blue_blocks = [(56,12), (56,16), (56,34), (56,38), (56,46), (56,50)]

for row, col in blue_blocks:
    result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': row, 'col': col}], intent='solve')
    print(f"Click ({row},{col}): state={state_graph.current}, completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
    
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ['c', 'N', 'p', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] <= 6:
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            print(f"  {n['color']} at ({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")
    
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed', False):
        print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
